seo.title: 15 Best Things To Do in Hat Yai (2026 Guide)
seo.metaDescription: Discover the 15 best things to do in Hat Yai in 2026. From floating markets and dim sum to waterfalls, night markets, and the famous reclining Buddha.

intro: Hat Yai isn't your typical Thai tourist destination — there are no beaches or ancient ruins here. Instead, southern Thailand's biggest city delivers a raw, multicultural experience driven by incredible food, bargain markets, and a uniquely Thai-Chinese-Malay atmosphere. Whether you're crossing from Malaysia for a weekend food binge or exploring Thailand's deep south, here are 15 things that make Hat Yai worth the trip.

activities.name: Walk Inside the Reclining Buddha at Wat Hat Yai Nai
activities.description: Southern Thailand's largest reclining Buddha stretches 35 meters long and rises 15 meters high — the third largest in all of Thailand. Unlike most Buddha statues you can only admire from outside, this one has an entrance at the back where you can walk inside the hollow structure. The temple grounds are peaceful with shaded gardens and merit-making pavilions.
tips: Enter the statue from the rear — there's a small doorway leading inside
Dress modestly with covered shoulders and knees

activities.name: Ride the Cable Car at Hat Yai Municipal Park
activities.description: The cable car at Hat Yai Municipal Park connects the 20-meter golden standing Buddha (Phra Buddha Mongkol Maharaj) to the Four-Faced Brahma (Thao Maha Phrom) statue, offering sweeping panoramic views of the city, surrounding hills, and on clear days, glimpses toward the Malaysian border.
activities.price: 200 THB (~$5.50 USD) adults, 100 THB children (round trip cable car)
tips: Visit late afternoon for softer light and cooler temperatures
The park itself is free — you only pay for the cable car

activities.name: Feast Your Way Through Kim Yong Market
activities.description: Hat Yai's most iconic market is a food and shopping wonderland open daily from 6 AM to 6 PM. The morning food section serves khao mok gai (chicken biryani), dim sum, and southern Thai breakfast dishes, while the shopping halls overflow with dried fruits, nuts, snacks, electronics, and household goods at wholesale prices.
activities.price: Free entry; budget 100–300 THB for food
tips: Morning (7–10 AM) is best for breakfast food; afternoon for shopping
Try the dried mangosteen and durian chips as souvenirs
activities.bestTime: Morning for food, afternoon for shopping

activities.name: Browse the Khlong Hae Floating Market
activities.description: Southern Thailand's first and only floating market comes alive on Friday through Sunday afternoons. Vendors dressed in traditional costumes sell southern Thai, Chinese, and Muslim food from wooden boats along the canal — roti, fish curries, grilled chicken, and tropical fruit smoothies in eco-friendly containers.
activities.price: Free entry; budget 100–200 THB for food
tips: Open Fri–Sun only, 3 PM to 8 PM — arrive early for best selection
Take a Grab from central Hat Yai (15–20 minutes, ~80 THB)

activities.name: Chase Waterfalls at Ton Nga Chang
activities.description: This stunning 7-tier waterfall 25 km west of Hat Yai earns its name ('elephant tusks') from twin cascades at the top that resemble ivory tusks. The lower tiers have natural swimming pools surrounded by pristine rainforest. The wildlife sanctuary is home to hornbills, gibbons, and rare flora.
tips: Tiers 1–3 are easy walks; tiers 4–7 require proper hiking shoes and fitness
Bring swimwear for the natural pools at tier 2 and 3

activities.name: Eat Authentic Hat Yai Fried Chicken
activities.description: Hat Yai-style fried chicken is so legendary it's named after the city in restaurants across Thailand. The secret is a marinade of dried spices — including turmeric and white pepper — before deep-frying to a golden, crackling skin. Served with sticky rice and crispy fried shallots, it's the city's signature dish. Find it at street stalls and dedicated fried chicken shops throughout town.
tips: Look for stalls with a queue of locals — they know the best ones
Pair with som tum (papaya salad) and sticky rice for the full southern Thai experience

activities.name: Start the Day with Dim Sum
activities.description: Hat Yai's large Thai-Chinese community maintains a dim sum breakfast culture rivaling any Chinatown in Southeast Asia. Dozens of dim sum restaurants open early and serve steamer baskets of har gow, siu mai, char siu bao, and congee at prices that would make Hong Kong jealous — most dishes 20–40 THB per basket.
tips: Chokdee Dim Sum is a favorite among locals and visitors
Dim Sum Chabura offers halal-certified dim sum — great for Muslim travelers

activities.name: Explore Greenway Night Market
activities.description: Hat Yai's biggest and most vibrant night market sprawls along Sripuwanart Road every evening from 5 PM. Hundreds of stalls serve grilled seafood, Thai crepes, fruit shakes, satay, and every southern Thai street food imaginable, alongside clothing, accessories, and electronics at rock-bottom prices.
activities.price: Free entry; budget 100–300 THB for food and shopping
tips: Arrive hungry — the food variety is the main attraction
Weekend evenings are most lively but also most crowded

activities.name: Visit the Ornate Wat Chue Chang
activities.description: This Chinese-Thai temple stands out for its extraordinary exterior covered in hundreds of thousands of ornate porcelain tiles. The architecture blends traditional Thai temple design with Chinese ceramic artistry, creating a dazzling, Instagram-worthy mosaic that's unique in southern Thailand.
tips: Best photographed in soft morning light when the tiles catch the sun
Combine with a walk to nearby Kim Yong Market

activities.name: Try Khao Mok Gai (Southern Chicken Biryani)
activities.description: Hat Yai's Muslim community brings its own signature dish — khao mok gai, a fragrant chicken biryani cooked with turmeric, cumin, and cardamom. Served with a rich broth dipping sauce, sweet pickled cucumber, and often fried shallots. Found at Muslim food stalls throughout the city, especially near the central mosque.
tips: Look for halal signs and Muslim-run stalls near Niphat Uthit 2 Road
Best for lunch — many stalls sell out by 2 PM

activities.name: Shop at Lee Garden Plaza and Central Festival
activities.description: Hat Yai has two major shopping malls that draw Malaysian bargain hunters. Lee Garden Plaza is the older landmark in the city center with fashion, electronics, and restaurants. Central Festival Hat Yai is the modern mega-mall with international brands, a cinema, and a massive food court.
tips: Central Festival connects directly to Centara Hotel — convenient for a lunch break
Use your passport for tourist VAT refund on purchases over 2,000 THB at a single store

activities.name: Day Trip to Songkhla Old Town
activities.description: Just 30 minutes east of Hat Yai, Songkhla Old Town offers a completely different experience: colorful street art murals, a charming Sino-Portuguese historic quarter, the Songkhla National Museum, and some of the best seafood restaurants in southern Thailand along the lakefront.
activities.price: 100 THB museum entrance, 200–400 THB for meals
tips: Hire a Grab from Hat Yai (30–40 min, ~200 THB) or take a songthaew (20 THB)
Don't miss the street art in the old town lanes — it's surprisingly extensive

activities.name: Taste Roti and Teh Tarik at Muslim Food Stalls
activities.description: Hat Yai's proximity to Malaysia means authentic roti (crispy flatbread) and teh tarik (pulled tea) are everywhere. Tamrab restaurant specializes in mataba — an unleavened fried bread stuffed with beef and spices. The Muslim food stalls near the train station serve roti canai with curry dipping sauce that rivals anything in Penang.
tips: Tamrab on Niphat Uthit Road is the most famous mataba spot
Try the roti with condensed milk for a sweet snack version

activities.name: Visit the Songkhla Folklore Museum
activities.description: Located in a beautifully restored Sino-Portuguese mansion, this museum showcases the cultural heritage of southern Thailand's diverse communities — Thai, Chinese, Malay, and sea gypsy. Exhibits include traditional costumes, shadow puppets (nang talung), and local crafts.
tips: Combine with a Songkhla Old Town day trip
The building itself is worth the visit for its architecture

activities.name: Cross the Border to Sadao for a Malaysia Day Trip
activities.description: The Sadao border crossing is just 50 km south of Hat Yai, making it easy to do a visa run or day trip into Malaysia. Many travelers combine a Hat Yai food weekend with a trip to Danok duty-free zone on the Thai side, or continue to Bukit Kayu Hitam on the Malaysian side.
activities.price: Minivan to border ~100 THB, taxi ~500 THB
tips: Bring your passport — obviously. Border is open daily 5 AM to 9 PM
The Danok duty-free zone on the Thai side sells cheap alcohol and perfume

travelTips: Download Grab before arriving — it's the most reliable and fair-priced transport in Hat Yai
Malaysian ringgit is widely accepted at markets, but you'll get better rates paying in baht
Hat Yai is a food city — skip the hotel breakfast and eat at Kim Yong Market or a dim sum house instead
Many shops and markets close by 6 PM, but night markets and food stalls keep the city alive until midnight
ATMs charge 220 THB per foreign withdrawal — withdraw larger amounts or use exchange booths
Friday to Sunday is busiest with Malaysian weekend visitors — expect crowded markets and higher hotel prices
The rainy season (Sep–Nov) can bring flash flooding — check weather before visiting
Songkhla Old Town (30 min away) offers beaches, street art, and seafood — don't miss it as a day trip
